tool_call_sequence.description: For an L0 diagnosis-only task, the agent must correctly document cluster headache as the primary diagnosis to enable appropriate acute and preventive management planning.

nl_assertions: States the most likely diagnosis is cluster headache and uses the term "cluster headache" explicitly.

Elicits key cluster headache features: strictly unilateral severe orbital/supraorbital/temporal pain, short duration attacks (typically 15–180 minutes), attack frequency (up to multiple/day), and circadian/cluster periodicity.

Asks about ipsilateral cranial autonomic symptoms (e.g., lacrimation, conjunctival injection, rhinorrhea/nasal congestion, eyelid edema, facial sweating) and/or restlessness/agitational behavior during attacks.

Screens for red flags suggesting secondary headache (e.g., first/worst sudden onset, new neurologic deficits, fever/meningismus, immunosuppression, cancer history, pregnancy/postpartum, age >50 with new headache, head trauma) and indicates escalation if present.

Differentiates from common mimics with at least brief comparison questions: migraine (photophobia/phonophobia, nausea, preference for lying still), trigeminal neuralgia (brief electric-shock pains, triggers), sinusitis/dental causes, and other TACs (paroxysmal hemicrania, SUNCT/SUNA).
Acknowledges familial occurrence is possible but does not overstate inheritance; avoids implying a guaranteed genetic transmission.
Meets communication milestone: explains the diagnosis in patient-friendly terms and includes what cluster headache is and why the presentation fits.
Meets communication milestone: explains standard acute treatments and includes both "oxygen" and a "triptan" as evidence-based abortive options (without necessarily prescribing at L0).

Mentions key safety checks that must occur before any prescribing: allergy check, contraindication check (especially for triptans), and drug interaction check; does not recommend triptan use without noting cardiovascular/vasospastic contraindications.
Provides a basic follow-up/safety-net: advises urgent evaluation if red flags occur or if pattern changes, and suggests follow-up for preventive strategy discussion if attacks recur.

minimal_paths.description: Identify classic cluster headache based on unilateral severe short attacks with autonomic signs and restlessness; explain diagnosis and evidence-based abortive options; document diagnosis.
steps: Elicit headache phenotype (location, severity, quality, duration, frequency, timing).
Ask about ipsilateral autonomic symptoms and restlessness/agitation during attacks.
Assess periodicity (daily timing, cluster periods) and triggers (e.g., alcohol during a bout).
Screen for secondary headache red flags and focal neurologic symptoms.
Explain diagnosis using the term "cluster headache" and why it fits.
Explain acute treatment options including high-flow oxygen and a triptan; note safety checks required before prescribing (allergies, contraindications, interactions).
Record diagnosis as cluster headache.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent first frames a short differential (migraine vs cluster vs trigeminal neuralgia) in conversation, then narrows to cluster headache with targeted questions and documents the diagnosis.
steps: Clarify key distinguishing features among migraine, trigeminal neuralgia, and TACs.
Confirm cluster headache features (short severe unilateral attacks, autonomic symptoms/restlessness, cluster periodicity).
Rule out red flags/secondary causes by history.
Explain diagnosis (must include "cluster headache").
Explain treatment options (must include "oxygen" and "triptan"), noting safety checks before prescribing.
Record diagnosis as cluster headache.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent emphasizes safety-netting and need for urgent assessment if atypical features exist, while still concluding cluster headache as most likely and documenting it.
steps: Take focused cluster headache history and autonomic symptom review.
Perform red-flag screen and explicitly state conditions that would warrant urgent evaluation/imaging.
Communicate diagnosis ("cluster headache") with rationale.
Discuss abortive treatments (oxygen, triptan) and the need for allergy/contraindication/interaction checks before prescribing.
Record diagnosis as cluster headache.
